facts.text: The Delaware, Lackawanna and Western Railroad, originally the Lackawanna and Western Railroad, operated from 1851 to 1960, shaping the city’s industrial growth.
The Lackawanna Historical Association, founded to promote original research, meets monthly in the Museum Room at the Lackawanna Public Library.
Father Nelson Baker, known as the "Padre of the Poor," built the Basilica of Our Lady of Victory, a landmark still serving thousands daily.
Smokes Creek, named after Seneca Chief Sayenqueraghta, winds through Lackawanna before emptying into Lake Erie.
Lackawanna sits just south of Buffalo on Lake Erie’s shore, making it part of the Greater Buffalo metropolitan area.
Before 1842, the area that is now Lackawanna was part of the Buffalo Creek Reservation, opened to settlement only after the Seneca sold it.
